channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: WonderCon’s Cosplay Raises The Game With Some Completely Stunning Fits
item.content:encoded:
WonderCon 2026 has come and gone, the colossal Anaheim convention now in its 39th year (give or take a Covid), and it continues to generate some of the best cosplay you’ll ever see. This year’s collection from our roving photographers at Minerablu features some of the most impressive fits we’ve seen in a long while, both extravagant and super-subtle.

Dispatch firmly establishes itself as a cosplayer’s dream game, and I think we’re going to be seeing a lot more from it throughout the year. Today we’ve got appearances from Blonde Blazer, Sonar and Coupé. There’s also your obligatory excellent Marvel Rivals costume and the undefeatable League of Legends, but also some really out-there picks as well. I cannot get over the Garrett the Garbage Man fit from the uncanny Jason Momoa look-a-like @aquaman__sd, which will make you do a double-take. But also that Eevee mascot outfit! It’s more professional than the professionals.

Even designs from incredibly common cosplay characters Kairi and Sora from Kingdom Hearts feel like a significant level-up from the norm, with both looking uncannily cartoon-like thanks to perfect wigs and makeup. And it’s just lovely to see eight different Mando players finding one another for a group photo.

As ever, you can see many more outfits from the convention in Mineralblu’s video below, or scroll down for the individual photographs.

Daredevil – Marvel Rivals

BioShock

Annihilus – Marvel

Ursula – The Little Mermaid

Spirit Blossom Syndra – League of Legends

Nico Robin – One Piece

Smokey and the Bandit

Garrett the Garbage Man – A Minecraft Movie

Megatron – Transformers

Gundam

Eevee – Pokémon

Sonar & Coupé – Dispatch

Drukhari – Warhammer 40K

Blonde Blazer – Dispatch

Mandolorians & Grogu

Magik – Marvel Rivals

Kairi & Sora – Kingdom Hearts

Watcher – Marvel



https://kotaku.com/wondercons-cosplay-raises-the-game-with-some-completely-stunning-fits-2000688984
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Fresh Leaks From The Legend Of Zelda Movie Are Spurring All Kinds Of Fan Theories
item.content:encoded:
Sony and Nintendo’s The Legend of Zelda film has wrapped shooting in New Zealand, and while we’re still just over a year out from its May 7, 2027 release, the crumbs of set photos and other leaks have given fans of Nintendo’s fantasy series a bit to speculate about. What little we’ve seen of the movie’s production doesn’t necessarily point to it being an adaptation of any one specific game, and some of the new images coming out of the set also suggest that it may draw on multiple sources. So maybe the film will follow the Super Mario movies’ lead and be a bit of an amalgamation of different Zelda stories? 

Gyula Pados, the director of photography on the film, posted a since-deleted Instagram post with a few images from the set. However, before it was taken down, fans saved all the images and are now dissecting them on social media. The most straightforward one worth talking about is the photo of the movie’s clapper, which gives us our first look at Benjamin Evan Ainsworth as Link in his full green tunic with the Master Sword in hand. The design looks pretty similar to Link’s Twilight Princess appearance with chainmail underneath the green top. Notably, he also seems to be wearing a cape of some kind on his back, which is reminiscent of one of his Tears of the Kingdom looks. So even in the character design, it looks like the Zelda movie is borrowing from multiple Zelda games.

A foto de uma claquete do filme The Legend of Zelda postada pelo cinematógrafo do filme, Gyulas Pados, parece exibir uma arte com o Ben como Link (não vista?) e também a Master Sword
—
via @ gyula_pados_dp (Instagram) pic.twitter.com/WoCTaGOcZX

— 'Necro' Felipe #UnivNintendo (@necrolipe) April 20, 2026

The other picture that fans are dissecting is a group shot of the film’s crew standing on the set. We can’t see much of the background, but there’s just enough visible in the shot for folks to think it might be the Temple of Time as seen in The Legend of Zelda: Ocarina of Time. It looks like there are engravings on the floor of the set that might resemble the Goddess Pearls from The Wind Waker as well. So what the hell is going on in the Legend of Zelda movie is anyone’s guess so far.

Yes https://t.co/HmtWmZ1UBq pic.twitter.com/xVzFjQDFoK

— 𝐙𝐄𝐋𝐃𝐂𝐀𝐒𝐓 (@ZeldcastPodcast) April 20, 2026

If you know anything about Zelda lore, you know this is (almost) all connected anyway, so it’s entirely possible the film is using series-wide worldbuilding as set dressing for an otherwise standalone story. A story synopsis was added to the movie’s IMDb page, but it was a pretty general description of a Zelda storyline and hasn’t appeared anywhere else, so take it with a grain of salt.

A live-action adaptation of the video game franchise. ‘The Legend of Zelda’ follows Link, a young warrior destined to protect the magical kingdom of Hyrule from the forces of darkness. The land is under threat from Ganon, a ruthless warlord who seeks the Triforce-an ancient relic said to grant limitless power. To stop him, Link must embark on a perilous journey, battling monstrous creatures, exploring treacherous dungeons, and solving intricate puzzles to uncover sacred artifacts that can aid him in his quest.

While Pados’ post was clearly sent out too early and without corporate approval, we’ll likely see more official promo and story clarification in the coming months now that the Zelda movie has wrapped filming. At this point, the only official casting we know of is Ainsworth as Link and Bo Bragason as Zelda, though some set photos have fans speculating that Severance actor Dichen Lachman may be playing Impa, a member of the Sheikah tribe and also typically Zelda’s advisor, or in some cases, bodyguard.



https://kotaku.com/legend-of-zelda-movie-set-photos-leak-link-temple-time-2000689021
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: PlayStation Veteran Shuhei Yoshida Says Jim Ryan Fired Him For Refusing To Do ‘Ridiculous Things’
item.content:encoded:
Shuhei Yoshida had been the president of Sony Computer Entertainment Worldwide Studios for over a decade when he was suddenly replaced in 2019 by Guerrilla Games studio head Hermen Hulst. It was one of a number of surprising shakeups at PlayStation as Jim Ryan took charge ahead of the launch of the PS5. Speaking at Australia’s 2026 ALT: Games festival over the weekend, Yoshida shed some more light on the nature of his demotion and his final years at Sony.

“I helped Santa Monica to make God of War, Naughty Dog to make Uncharted and The Last of Us, and Sucker Punch to make the beautiful Ghost of Tsushima,” he said in a speech at the event, This Week In Video Games reports. “Ghost of Tsushima was one of the last games that I worked on as the president of Worldwide Studios. But in 2019, after 11 years leading the first-party development, I was fired from the role.”

“Jim Ryan wanted to remove me from first-party because I didn’t listen to him,” he added with a smile and to reported laughter in the room. “He asked to do some ridiculous things, and I said ‘No.’”

While Yoshida didn’t go into further detail on the nature of their disagreements, Ryan’s time as CEO at PlayStation saw the console maker pursue big acquisitions and large investments in live-service games. Many of the studios purchased have been closed or faced layoffs, and many of the live-service games have been canceled or failed at launch.

The most infamous example of this strategy, the hero shooter Concord, was so bad that it was quickly “unreleased,” and the studio behind it, Firewalk, shuttered just a couple of years after being bought. Former Chairman of SIE Worldwide Studios Shawn Layden departed the company in 2019, just prior to Yoshida’s demotion, with no real explanation at the time. He later insinuated he was also a casualty of the new live-service push.

“To be honest, you know, the company was making some strategic decisions about where they want to take the platform in the future with a heavy emphasis on games as a service, live-service gaming, subscription formulas, recurring revenue, whatnot, and that was kind of not my wheelhouse,” Layden said in a podcast interview last year.

Unlike Layden, Yoshida stayed on at Sony for the first half of the PS5 generation to help support indie game development on the platform. A familiar face at gaming conventions, scouting for promising projects and the small teams behind them, Yoshida said that since his official retirement in 2025, he’s essentially doing the same work “evangelizing indie games” as he was in his final years at Sony, just with a chance to consult on Nintendo and Xbox’s platforms as well.



https://kotaku.com/sony-ps5-playstation-shuhei-yoshida-jim-ryan-fired-2000688973
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
